starter.coreIdea: If duplicates exist, the number of unique values is smaller than the original list. Collapsing the numbers into a set removes duplicates, so comparing sizes answers the question.
starter.pattern: Hash Set / uniqueness check. When you need to know whether you've "seen something before", reach for a hash set — membership checks and insertions are both `O(1)` on average.
starter.standardApproach: Insert each number into a set while scanning. If a number is already present, you've found a duplicate and can return early. Otherwise the whole array is unique.
starter.whyItWorks: A set guarantees uniqueness, so the first time an insert collides with an existing element you have proof of a duplicate. The early return avoids scanning the rest of the array.
starter.timeComplexity: O(n) — a single pass over the input.
starter.spaceComplexity: O(n) — the set may hold every element.

starter.coreIdea: For each number `x`, the partner you need is `target - x`. Instead of searching for that partner with a second loop, remember every number you've already seen in a hash map so the lookup is instant.
starter.pattern: Hash Map / complement lookup. The canonical "trade space for time" trick: an `O(n^2)` brute force collapses to `O(n)` by caching what you've seen.
starter.standardApproach: Walk the array once. For each element compute its complement and check the map; if present, return both indices. Otherwise store the current value and index.
starter.whyItWorks: By the time you reach element `i`, every earlier element is in the map. So if a valid pair ends at `i`, its partner was already recorded and is found in `O(1)`.
starter.timeComplexity: O(n) — one pass with constant-time map operations.
starter.spaceComplexity: O(n) — up to n entries in the map.
similarProblems: Two Sum II - Input Array Is Sorted

starter.coreIdea: Two strings are anagrams iff they contain the same characters with the same frequencies. Count letters in one string, then subtract while scanning the other — everything should cancel to zero.
starter.pattern: Frequency counting with a hash map (or a fixed-size array for a known alphabet).
starter.standardApproach: Build a frequency table for the first string and compare it to the frequency table of the second. For a lowercase-only alphabet a 26-length array keeps it `O(1)` space.
starter.whyItWorks: Incrementing for `s` and decrementing for `t` means equal frequencies cancel out. Any leftover non-zero count proves a mismatch.
starter.timeComplexity: O(n) — one pass over both strings.
starter.spaceComplexity: O(1) for the fixed 26-letter array (O(k) for a general alphabet of size k).
similarProblems: Find All Anagrams in a String

best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock.title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

starter.coreIdea: Track the lowest price seen so far (your best buy day). For each later day, compute the profit if you sold today and keep the maximum.
starter.pattern: Sliding Window / single-pass greedy. Treat `left` as the buy day and `right` as the sell day; whenever a new minimum appears, slide `left` to it.
starter.standardApproach: Maintain a running minimum price and a running best profit. For every price, update the minimum, then update the best profit with `price - min`.
starter.keyCode:
def maxProfit(prices: list[int]) -> int:
    min_price = float("inf")
    best = 0
    for price in prices:
        min_price = min(min_price, price)
        best = max(best, price - min_price)
    return best
starter.whyItWorks: You never need to look back: once a cheaper buy price appears, the old one is strictly worse for every future sell day, so keeping only the minimum is sufficient.
similarProblems: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II
Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock with Cooldown

starter.coreIdea: Brackets must close in the reverse order they opened — that "last opened, first closed" rule is exactly a stack. Push opens, and on each closer make sure it matches the most recent open.
starter.pattern: Stack — for nested / balanced structure problems.
starter.standardApproach: Scan the string. Push opening brackets; on a closing bracket, pop and verify it matches. A valid string ends with an empty stack.
starter.whyItWorks: The stack always holds the still-open brackets in order. A closer is only valid against the top; an empty stack on a closer or a non-empty stack at the end signals imbalance.
starter.timeComplexity: O(n) — each character is pushed/popped at most once.
starter.spaceComplexity: O(n) — worst case all opening brackets.

starter.coreIdea: On a sorted array, comparing with the middle element lets you discard half the search space every step. Keep an inclusive `[left, right]` window and shrink it.
starter.pattern: Binary Search — halving a monotonic search space.
starter.standardApproach: Use an inclusive window. Compute mid, compare to target, and move `left` or `right` past mid. Stop when the window is empty.
starter.whyItWorks: Sortedness guarantees that everything left of mid is smaller and everything right is larger, so each comparison safely eliminates half the candidates.
starter.timeComplexity: O(log n) — the window halves each iteration.
similarProblems: Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array

starter.coreIdea: Generate every possible subset by making a choice for each number: include it or skip it.
starter.pattern: Backtracking — explore an implicit decision tree, adding and removing one element at a time as you recurse.
starter.standardApproach: Use DFS/backtracking: append the current path to the result, then explore choices from the next index onward, undoing each choice (pop) as you backtrack.
starter.whyItWorks: Each recursive path represents one subset, and moving the start index forward prevents duplicate ordering.
starter.timeComplexity: O(n * 2^n) — there are 2^n subsets and copying each costs up to O(n).
starter.spaceComplexity: O(n) recursion depth excluding output, O(n * 2^n) including the output list.
